summary: Updated the root Makefile link rule to write the object-file list into build/o_files and pass it to the linker via an @response-file style argument, avoiding printing or passing an extremely long $(O_FILES) string directly on the command line. Also added build.bat and clean.bat to .gitignore.
what_changed: In Makefile, changed the $(ELF) link recipe from directly invoking $(LD) with $(O_FILES) expanded inline to first writing $(O_FILES) into build/o_files using '@echo $(O_FILES) > build/o_files'.
In Makefile, changed the linker invocation to use '@build/o_files' instead of expanding the object list directly: '$(LD) $(LDFLAGS) -o $@ -lcf $(LDSCRIPT) @build/o_files'.
In .gitignore, added build.bat and clean.bat to the ignored IDE/local-helper files section.
smart_moves: Used a response-file-like linker argument to keep the generated command shorter and avoid console spam from very long object-file lists.
Preserved the existing link inputs and dependency rule, keeping $(ELF) dependent on $(O_FILES) and $(LDSCRIPT) while only changing how object paths are delivered to the linker.
Prefixed the object-list echo with '@' so the long expanded $(O_FILES) assignment is not printed by make, matching the PR rationale that long strings in console output could become problematic.
decomp_lessons: As decomp projects accumulate many source and object files, build rules should avoid expanding enormous file lists directly in visible shell commands.
For large object lists, writing file paths to a temporary list and passing that list to the linker can be a more scalable pattern than direct command-line expansion.
Small build-system changes can be important future-proofing even when they do not affect matched code or symbols.
naming_conventions: Temporary object-list file was named 'build/o_files', matching the existing Makefile variable name 'O_FILES' while placing the generated file under the build directory.
Ignored local batch helper scripts use simple root-level names 'build.bat' and 'clean.bat'.
follow_up_queries: Look for later Makefile changes involving build/o_files or response-file linker arguments to see whether this pattern persisted.
Search for build failures or platform-specific issues involving '@build/o_files' response files after PR #123.
Check whether build.bat and clean.bat were later introduced as documented local Windows helper scripts.
